comments.body: I suggest that you use Calendar::set
Any reason for using Calendars? Are you allowed to use java 8's time api?
But I want to change Date,no fixed .What else can I do?
see my comment below your question
Just here to say it's using StackOverflow in pretty bad faith to make minimal edits to your post to bump it to the front page again.
@SebastiaanvandenBroek Was going through some old answers and cleaning them up. In any case thought the order was based on number of votes not last update. Thanks.

answers.body:
If you want to initialize Date instances from a formatted string with both date and time then time codes should be added to the SimpleDateFormat pattern to parse strings in that format.

SimpleDateFormat sdf = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss");
Date startDate = sdf.parse("2018-03-28 00:00:00");
Date endDate   = sdf.parse("2018-03-28 23:59:59");

If you want to set the hour, minute, and second on the current date then use a Calendar instance and set fields on it accordingly.

 Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
 cal.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 0);
 cal.set(Calendar.MINUTE, 0);
 cal.set(Calendar.SECOND, 0);
 cal.set(Calendar.MILLISECOND, 0)
 Date startDate = cal.getTime();

 cal.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 23);
 cal.set(Calendar.MINUTE, 59);
 cal.set(Calendar.SECOND, 59);
 cal.set(Calendar.MILLISECOND, 999)
 Date endDate = cal.getTime();

Next output the Date in a particular format:

SimpleDateFormat sdf = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy/MM/dd HH:mm:ss");
System.out.println(sdf.format(startDate));
System.out.println(sdf.format(endDate));

Output:

2018/03/28 00:00:00
2018/03/28 23:59:59

Dealing with time zones

If time zone is other than the local time zone then it's a good idea to be explicit with what timezone you're working with. Calendar and SimpleDateFormat instances must be consistent with what timezone you're dealing with or the date and/or times may be off.

TimeZone utc = TimeZone.getTimeZone("UTC");
Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance(utc);
SimpleDateFormat sdf = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy/MM/dd HH:mm:ss");
sdf.setTimeZone(utc);

A substitute for SimpleDateFormat is using DateTimeFormatter class found in the newer java.time package added to Java 8.

Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
    SimpleDateFormat sdf1 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 00:00:00");
    SimpleDateFormat sdf2 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 23:59:59");

    SimpleDateFormat sdf3 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss");

    String str1=sdf1.format(cal.getTime());
    String str2=sdf2.format(cal.getTime());
    try {
        Date startDate = sdf3.parse(str1);
        Date endDate = sdf3.parse(str2);
        System.out.println(str1);
        System.out.println(str2);

        System.out.println(startDate.toString());
        System.out.println(endDate.toString());
    } catch (ParseException e) {
        // TODO Auto-generated catch block
        e.printStackTrace();
    }

OUT PUT
2018-03-28 00:00:00
2018-03-28 23:59:59
Wed Mar 28 00:00:00 ICT 2018
Wed Mar 28 23:59:59 ICT 2018

I think, maybe sdf1 and sdf2 don't provide clear format. So change time to HH:mm:ss.

You can solve this problem like this

Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
    SimpleDateFormat sdf1 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 00:00:00");
    SimpleDateFormat sdf2 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 23:59:59");
    String str1=sdf1.format(cal.getTime());
    String str2=sdf2.format(cal.getTime());
    Date startDate = sdf1.parse(str1);
    Date endDate = sdf2.parse(str2);
    String startDateTime  = sdf1.format(startDate);
    String endDateTime = sdf2.format(endDate);
    System.out.println("startDate ----->" + startDateTime);
    System.out.println("endDate ----->" + endDateTime);

The output of this 

startDate ----->2018-03-28 00:00:00
endDate ----->2018-03-28 23:59:59

Hope this is what you want.

comments.body: According to Javadocs Use set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 0) to reset the hour value.

answers.body:
If you just want to get the Date values for today's start and end times, you don't need to use date formatting utilities (like SimpleDateFormat) at all:

Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
cal.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 0);
cal.clear(Calendar.MINUTE);
cal.clear(Calendar.SECOND);
cal.clear(Calendar.MILLISECOND);
Date startDate = cal.getTime();
cal.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 23);
cal.set(Calendar.MINUTE, 59);
cal.set(Calendar.SECOND, 59);
cal.set(Calendar.MILLISECOND, 999);
Date endDate = cal.getTime();

tl;dr

You cannot use a formatter to set a time in your date-time object. 00:00:00 and 23:59:59 are not valid formatting codes, and do not affect the time-of-day of either your Calendar or your java.util.Date object.

Instead, (a) establish your date-time objects with the values you want, (b) then generate text output afterwards. Two separate steps.

Solution

Use java.time classes for such work. Never use legacy classes Date, Calendar, SimpleDateFormat.

ZoneId z = ZoneId.of( "Africa/Tunis" ) ;
LocalDate today = LocalDate.now( z ) ;
ZonedDateTime begin = today.atStartOfDay( z ) ; // Let java.time determine when a day starts. 
ZonedDateTime end = today.plusDays( 1 ).atStartOfDay( z ) ;

With date-time objects in hand, generate text.

System.out.println( "Today: " + begin + "/" + end ) ;

Today: 2025-11-01T00:00+01:00[Africa/Tunis]/2025-11-02T00:00+01:00[Africa/Tunis]

Or, specify a format. Apparently you want to show only date and time. The easiest way is to use the predefined ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME formatter, then swap out T from the middle for a SPACE character.

String output = 
    begin.format( DateTimeFormatter.ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME ).replace( "T" , " " ) 
    + "/" 
    + end.format( DateTimeFormatter.ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME ).replace( "T" , " " ) ;

See this code run at Ideone.com.

2025-11-01 00:00:00/2025-11-02 00:00:00

If you really want your 23:59:59 value, subtract a second. But then your result will not be representing the full day, your result will be a second short of a full day.

end = end.minusSeconds( 1 ) ;  // 23:59:59. Do not do this, as you miss a second from the day.

Problems in your code

  • You tried to set time-of-day by way of a formatter rather than establishing your date-time values ahead of formatting.
  • You used terribly flawed date-time classes that are now legacy. Never use Date, Calendar, SimpleDateFormat, etc. Use only the modern java.time classes built into Java 8+.
  • You assumed the day begins and ends at certain times. Some dates in some time zones start or end at other times, for example, starting at 01:00. Always let java.time determine the beginning and ending times for a particular date in a particular time zone.
  • You ignored time zones, a crucial issue.
  • You omit the last second between 23:59:59 and 00:00:00. Troubles ensue: This would ruin queries to a database, your day length would be off by a second, etc.

Time zone

The start and end of a day varies around the globe by time zone.

A time zone name is defined in Continent/Region format. For example Europe/Berlin and Pacific/Auckland.

ZoneId z = ZoneId.of( "America/Edmonton" );

Half-Open

Usually it is best to define a span of time as Half-Open. The beginning is inclusive while the ending is exclusive.

So a day begins at its first moment, and runs up to, but does not include, the first moment of the following day.

LocalDate

Represent a date with java.time.LocalDate class. This class means a year, month, and day.

LocalDate date = LocalDate.of( 2018, Month.MARCH , 28 );

ZonedDateTime

Represent a point on the time line, as seen in a particular time zone, using java.time.ZonedDateTime.

Let java.time determine the start of the day.

ZoneId z = ZoneId.of( "Asia/Tokyo" );
ZonedDateTime begin = date.atStartOfDay( z ) ;

Under Half-Open approach, the day ends before the first moment of the following day. So add one to the date to move to next day.

LocalDate nextDay = date.plusDays( 1 );

Let java.time determine the beginning of that day in that zone.

ZonedDateTime end = nextDay.atStartOfDay( z) ;

Compare

You can test to see if a moment landed on that day.

Tip… Regarding the start, testing for “is the moment NOT before the start” is briefer than asking “is the moment equal to or later than the start”,

ZonedDateTime zdt = ZonedDateTime.of( 2018, 3 , 28 , 23 , 59 , 59 , 500_000_000 , z ) ;
if ( ( ! zdt.isBefore( begin ) ) && zdt.isBefore( end ) ) { … same day … }

This example shows how your code would fail this test, having skipped the last full second of the day. Using Half-Open instead includes that last full second.

If you do much of this date-range work, I recommend adding the ThreeTen-Extra library to your project.

Duration

To show that we have captured the entire day, without losing a second as your code did, calculate the duration.

Duration duration = Duration.between( begin , end ) ;

Legacy

If you must produce a java.util.Date, to interoperate with old code not yet updated to java.time, use the new conversion methods added to the old classes.

java.util.Date juDateBegin = Date.from( begin.toInstant() ) ;

title: How to set datetime to end of day in java
body:
I want to set datetime of day as: startDate=2018/03/28 00:00:00 and endDate=2018/03/28 23:59:59

    Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
    SimpleDateFormat sdf1 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 00:00:00");
    SimpleDateFormat sdf2 = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd 23:59:59");
    String str1=sdf1.format(cal.getTime());
    String str2=sdf2.format(cal.getTime());
    Date startDate = sdf1.parse(str1);
    Date endDate = sdf2.parse(str2);

My problem:program is working and output endDate=2018/03/28 00:00:00

Would you please point out any mistakes to me in code?

update: i used debug and it's working correct with 

String str2=sdf2.format(cal.getTime());//2018-03-28 23:59:59

but when change string==>date is not correct with output 2018/03/28 00:00:00
